registry.plantingRegime: 100% Sown. Mix: 40% Cocksfoot, 30% Timothy, 20% Creeping Red Fescue, 10% Red Clover. Density: 25 kg/ha. SPH: N/A. 6m width provides enhanced sediment trapping capacity over the standard 3m Grass Silt Buffer.

registry.waterImpacts: Enhanced Sediment Trap: Double the width of standard Grass Silt Buffer increases particle residence time and trapping capacity. registry.soilTypeCondition: Arable field margins and ditch edges where sub-surface tile drainage infrastructure is present. Suitable for Land Classes 1–4.2. Specifically designed for locations where deep-rooting species must be avoided to protect buried drains.
registry.plantingRegime: 2 Rows (staggered). Mix: 40% Goat Willow (Salix caprea), 30% Grey Willow (Salix cinerea), 30% Guelder Rose (Viburnum opulus). Density: 3 plants/m staggered. SPH: ~4,000. Water-tolerant species selected for wet margin compatibility.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1–2: Spot weed and mulch mat for establishment. Year 3: Remove mulch mats. Long-term: 3–4 year rotational side-trim (December–February) to maintain structural density at 1.5–2.5m height. Biomass: Chipped in situ or removed. No root disturbance — avoid deep cultivation adjacent to established plants.
registry.rootArchitecture: Shallow Fibrous: Goat and Grey Willow produce dense lateral root mats (0–30cm) specifically designed to avoid penetrating deep buried drainage assets. Root spread does not exceed 0.5–0.7× canopy width at managed heights. Guelder Rose fibrous root system remains entirely within the upper soil horizon. Safe stand-off from tile drain runs verified at standard 1m spacing.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Pollinator Hub: Goat Willow provides the earliest high-quality pollen source for emerging queen bumblebees (March). Guelder Rose provides late-season red berries for fieldfares and redwings. Willow catkins support >50 invertebrate species.
registry.waterImpacts: Silt Filter: Dense multi-stem base creates physical turbulence trapping overland flow particles. Willow root mats bind ditch and field margin soil, preventing bank slumping and reducing turbidity. Does not intercept subsurface nitrate flows (shallow roots).
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Woodland Carbon Code (WCC) Native Broadleaf/Pine Yield Classes. Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Zero aerodynamic windbreak value; zero shadow drag.
registry.agronomicUtility: Infrastructure Protection: Delivers biodiversity compliance without risk to expensive tile drainage networks. Maintains field edge trafficability. Willow provides modest biomass resource if height is managed to permit harvesting.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Biodiversity Strip compliant. Suitable as EFA Buffer Strip. Species mix approved under NatureScot native species list.
registry.additionality: Drain-Safe Biodiversity: Proves >3 native species planted at verified safe stand-off distance from sub-surface drainage infrastructure, beyond what a standard fallow or bare margin baseline provides.

registry.soilTypeCondition: East Neuk coastal arable field margins and sheltered inland boundaries within economically viable distance of distillery and botanical product supply chains. Suitable for Land Classes 1–3 on free-draining sandy loams and coastal clay-loams.
registry.plantingRegime: 2 Rows (staggered). Mix: 40% Hazel (Corylus avellana), 20% Elder (Sambucus nigra), 20% Dog Rose (Rosa canina), 20% Blackthorn (Prunus spinosa). Density: 3 plants/m. SPH: ~4,500. Focus on late-season fruiting and nectar-rich species for maximum foraging value.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1–2: Spot weed and mulch mat. Year 3: Remove mulch mats. Long-term: Annual selective winter prune (November–February) to maintain open fruiting structure and manage height at 1.5–2.5m. Hand-harvest of hazelnuts, elderflowers, elderberries, hips, and sloes as appropriate to the biome economic context (distillery, botanical, upland game). Biomass: Prunings chipped in situ.
registry.rootArchitecture: Heart-Roots: Elder and Hazel develop mid-depth heart-root systems (30–60cm) stabilising field margins without penetrating drainage infrastructure. Dog Rose and Blackthorn provide dense fibrous surface mat (0–20cm) binding headland soils and preventing rill erosion at field edges.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Foraging & Nesting Hub: Hazel provides high-protein nuts for dormice and squirrels; Blackthorn provides the primary early nectar source for overwintered bumblebees and solitary bees; Dog Rose provides dense thorny nesting structure for Yellowhammer and Linnet; Elder flowers and berries support over 70 invertebrate species and provide critical autumn food for migratory thrushes.
registry.waterImpacts: Field Margin Stabilisation: Dense basal multi-stem structure slows overland flow, intercepting coarse sediment and reducing rill erosion at field boundaries. Moderate N/P uptake through seasonal growth cycle. Not a primary nitrate interception feature — for dissolved nitrate management install adjacent to Variant 8 or 11.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Wilder Carbon Standard (WCS) soil baseline. Rapid AM fungi SOC lock, capped at Year 15 saturation. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~4m - 6m (Dense Porous Shrub). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Optimal porous windbreak. 50-75m protection. Reduced shadow compared to mature trees.
registry.agronomicUtility: Diversified Revenue: Direct sale of botanicals, fruits, and nuts to distilleries, food businesses, or farm shops generates secondary income. Dense structural base provides pest regulation habitat for ground beetles, parasitic wasps, and hoverflies, suppressing aphid and slug pressure in adjacent arable.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Biodiversity Strip compliant. EFA Buffer Strip eligible. Contributes to farm Biodiversity Net Gain assessment. NatureScot-approved native species throughout.
registry.additionality: Productive Foraging: Proves >4 native species with active economic harvest plan providing verified secondary income, demonstrating significant additionality over a standard unmanaged grass margin baseline.

catchmentHydrology.hydrologicalFunction: Root systems improve infiltration; aggressive coppice rotation reduces canopy interception more than Biomass Coppice.

waterPurification.biologicalJustification: Fast growth extracts nutrients but pyrolysis harvest removes biomass from site, limiting long-term soil accumulation. Biochar amendment to adjacent fields compensates via soil pathway.

registry.soilTypeCondition: NVZ hotspots; riparian ditch proximity, heavy clay-loams prone to leaching.
registry.plantingRegime: Triple-row. Mix: 40% Hawthorn, 30% Elder, 30% Common Reed (base). Density: 6 plants/m (staggered). SPH: ~6800.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: High weed suppression. Years 2+: 3-year mechanical coppice cycle. Harvest output: woodchip for farm boilers, district heat networks, or malt co-op supply. Note: fresh SRC willow at harvest carries 50-60% moisture. Pyrolysis (biochar) requires <20% MC, which is not reliably achievable on a 3-year cycle in the Scottish maritime climate without dedicated drying infrastructure. This variant produces combustion biomass fuel only. For biochar production see Variant 18 (minimum 6-year rotation required).
registry.rootArchitecture: Bio-Filter: Triple-row density creates a dense subsurface root wall; Elder heart-roots stabilize mid-depths while reeds intercept surface flow.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Filter Hub: Focus on aquatic insect host species. Dense basal structure provides cover for amphibians and nesting birds.
registry.waterImpacts: High N-Extraction: Rapid nutrient uptake from field runoff, serving as a physical and biological barrier protecting the ditch.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Woodland Carbon Code (WCC) Native Broadleaf/Pine Yield Classes. Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~4m - 6m (Dense Porous Shrub). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Optimal porous windbreak. 50-75m protection. Reduced shadow compared to mature trees.
registry.agronomicUtility: Fuel Savings & Soil Health: Root drying effect saves £70-£209/ha in tractor fuel. Biomass combustion yield 8-12 t DM/ha/yr. Revenue from biomass fuel supply to farm boiler or local network. Generates Nitrate Credits via N/P extraction. Carbon: fuel substitution credit accrues to energy system (not land carbon account).
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Water Margin / SEPA Security.
registry.additionality: Active Export: Verified annual N-export audit vs standard passive grass buffer, proving direct nutrient removal.

bespokePlantingRegime: Heavy nutrient pump species. 50% Alder (nitrogen fixing/trapping), 50% Willow. 3000 SPH. Wet soil tolerance.

airFiltration.biologicalFunction: Moderate-dense leaf area from Alder/Willow canopy; effective local air filtration.

catchmentHydrology.hydrologicalFunction: Designed for subsurface water interception and aquifer recharge rather than surface flood attenuation. Wet soil conditions maximise root-zone N/P uptake.

waterPurification.biologicalJustification: Maximum purification: Alder Frankia N-fixation cycles atmospheric N, Willow deep roots pump subsurface nitrate. In NVZ context this is the highest water quality intervention in the database. Mature canopy shading reduces algal growth.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Riparian margins, seasonally wet field corners, and ditch-adjacent positions with high nitrate loading from adjacent arable land. NVZ-designated land. Suitable for gleyed soils, wet clay-loams, and permanently moist alluvial soils where standing water is present for at least part of the year. Not suitable for freely-draining soils where wet-soil Alder will fail to establish.
registry.plantingRegime: 2 Rows (staggered). Mix: 50% Alder (Alnus glutinosa), 50% Willow (Salix viminalis or Salix fragilis). Density: 3,000 SPH. Spacing: 1m x 0.6m staggered. Select Fife local provenance Alder where available. Wet-soil tolerant planting stock essential — containerised or bare-root from specialist native nursery.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: Weed suppression around stems; no cultivation. Year 2–3: Remove dead stock and replace failures. Long-term: No routine harvesting required. Feature is managed as a permanent nitrate interception buffer. Optional management: light coppice of willow every 7–10 years if height management required for adjacent field operations. Alder is NOT harvested — permanent canopy maintained for maximum N-fixation. Biomass from any willow coppice: chipped for local biomass use.
registry.rootArchitecture: Deep Lateral N-Fixers: Alder hosts Frankia alni actinobacteria in root nodules throughout the shallow and mid-depth soil profile (0–60cm), fixing 40–150 kg atmospheric nitrogen per hectare per year. Willow develops extensive lateral root mats (0–40cm) with high transpiration capacity, actively pumping excess soil moisture and dissolved nitrate from the saturated root zone. Combined root system creates a persistent biological barrier intercepting subsurface nitrate leachate pathways.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Wetland Edge Habitat: Alder and Willow provide year-round riparian structure — Willow catkins are the primary early-season pollen source for bumblebees; Alder provides cone seed for siskins and redpolls; wet soil conditions support water vole, frog, newt, and aquatic invertebrate populations. Canopy shading of the adjacent watercourse helps maintain summer water temperature below critical thresholds for aquatic life.
registry.waterImpacts: Maximum Nitrate Interception: Alder root nodule N-fixation prevents net nitrate leaching from the buffer zone itself; Willow lateral roots actively pump dissolved nitrate from subsurface leachate pathways; wet soil denitrification by anaerobic bacteria in the saturated root zone converts residual nitrate to nitrogen gas. Combined mechanisms achieve near-100% nitrate interception efficiency in the riparian position (sepaMetrics nitrateRetention=100). This is the highest-performance NVZ compliance feature in the database.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: WCC SRC/wet-woodland profile. Permanent Alder root and SOC accumulation; above-ground Alder biomass not harvested. N-fixation provides additional SOC via soil organic nitrogen pathway. Mandatory 15% permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland GDD profile applied.
registry.agronomicUtility: NVZ Compliance: Primary NVZ nitrate compliance feature. Generates Nitrate Credits via verified N-uptake and denitrification. Reduces risk of SEPA enforcement action. Supports WFD Good Ecological Status progression for the Dreel Burn. Adjacent soil N-fixation reduces fertiliser requirement on adjoining fields over time.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Water Margin / Riparian Buffer Strip. SEPA NVZ compliance tool. ONCRA Nitrate Credit Union eligible. Aligns with WFD Article 11 Programme of Measures for NVZ catchments.
registry.additionality: Biological N-Interception: Frankia-mediated atmospheric nitrogen fixation provides verified biological N removal mechanism entirely absent from standard grass margin or fallow baseline. Denitrification in saturated root zone provides additional N-removal pathway with verifiable soil moisture monitoring.

bespokePlantingRegime: 3-Tier Climax Canopy. 60% Canopy (Scots Pine, Sessile Oak, Sycamore), 20% Understory (Rowan, Hazel), 20% Shrub Edge. 2500 SPH.

airFiltration.biologicalFunction: Massive 3D Leaf Area Index (LAI) and canopy roughness maximizes atmospheric PM2.5 deposition.

catchmentHydrology.hydrologicalFunction: High surface roughness and root infiltration slows overland flow effectively.

waterPurification.biologicalJustification: Deep native root systems intercept groundwater nutrients; provides excellent water shading.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Variable soils (clay to loam); targeted for low-yield corners or long-term nature link corridors.
registry.plantingRegime: Core Woodland. Mix: 40% Alder, 30% Downy Birch, 30% Wild Cherry. Density: 2m x 2m spacing. SPH: ~1600.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1-3: Vole guards and spot weeding. Long-term: Open canopy grass filter management with zero tillage. Biomass: Left for natural deadwood decay.

registry.biodiversityImpacts: Natural Arc: Permanent exclusion of intensive tillage creates a stable, long-term nature corridor for woodland specialist species.
registry.waterImpacts: High N-Sink: Deep roots intercept leached nitrates, acting as a permanent, long-term sequestration sponge for field runoff.

registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1-5: Vole guards and formative prune. Long-term: High-pruning (to 2m clear stem) to ensure constant ground-level airflow.

registry.biodiversityImpacts: Thermogenesis Corridor: Connects isolated pasture habitats for bats and avian predators.
registry.waterImpacts: Moisture Regulation: Open canopy and high airflow ensure rapid drying of the ground layer, preventing waterlogged "poaching" zones.

registry.additionality: Managed Micro-Climate: Active high-pruning protocol proves additionality over "plant and forget" blocks that create damp disease vectors.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Improved pasture and dairy grazing land, particularly south- and west-facing fields exposed to summer afternoon sun. Suitable for Land Classes 2–4 on loamy to clay-loam pasture soils. Priority locations: dairy units with documented summer heat stress indicators (reduced milk yield, somatic cell count increases, reduced conception rates in July–August).
registry.plantingRegime: Open-grown single or double row. Mix: 40% Sycamore (Acer pseudoplatanus), 40% Pedunculate Oak (Quercus robur), 20% Hornbeam (Carpinus betulus). Density: 1 tree per 5–6m. SPH: ~350–400. Wide spacing essential to maximise individual crown development and ground-level light penetration to maintain pasture productivity beneath canopy.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1–5: Vole guards, tree stakes, and spot weed suppression around base. Year 3–7: Formative pruning to develop high crown (clear stem to 2–2.5m) ensuring unimpeded airflow and livestock access to shade without bark damage. Long-term: Annual inspection for storm damage; remove epicormic growth from lower stem. Biomass: Timber asset managed under long-term schedule; no routine harvest. Root-blading optional at Year 10+ if pasture productivity under canopy requires renovation.
registry.rootArchitecture: Deep Taproots: Oak develops a primary taproot to 1.5m+ depth in well-structured soils, bypassing the shallow grass root zone and drawing deep groundwater via hydraulic redistribution during summer drought. Sycamore develops a heart-root system to 80–100cm. Hornbeam lateral roots provide mid-depth pasture stabilisation. Combined deep root architecture maximises hydraulic lift to maintain adjacent pasture soil moisture during the critical July–August drought period in the East Neuk.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Thermogenesis Corridor: Mature oak canopy supports 280+ associated invertebrate species — the highest of any native tree. Hollow trunks and dead wood in mature trees provide bat roost opportunities. Open-grown sycamore provides significant aphid resource for insectivorous birds. Connects isolated pasture habitats for barn owls, bats, and avian predators hunting rodents in the field margin.
registry.waterImpacts: Drought Buffering: Deep taproot hydraulic redistribution maintains pasture soil moisture during summer drought, reducing crop and pasture stress (see droughtResilience score=80). Canopy cooling (3.75°C below ambient on hot days; Burschel & Huss 1997) reduces evapotranspiration from the shaded zone and reduces livestock heat stress. Shade also reduces soil moisture loss through surface evaporation in the shade zone.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: WCC native broadleaf yield class profile (Sessile Oak/Sycamore mix). Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% permanence buffer deducted. Open-grown individual trees produce higher per-tree carbon stocks than woodland density, but strip-scale carbon is lower per metre than closed-canopy woodland at equivalent width. GDD: standard Scottish lowland profile.
registry.agronomicUtility: Animal Health and Productivity: Shade access reduces dairy cattle heat stress above Thermal Humidity Index 22°C, maintaining milk yield and reproductive performance during summer. Documented productivity improvements of 5–15% in milk yield on hot days when shade is available (AHDB Dairy heat stress guidance). Reduces somatic cell counts associated with heat stress mastitis risk. Long-term timber asset from Oak.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Agroforestry creation grant eligible. UKWAS pasture with trees category (where canopy cover <20%). Long-term WCC registration possible for individual trees once canopy area reaches minimum threshold.
registry.additionality: Active Drought Adaptation: Open-grown deep taproot species providing hydraulic redistribution in an exposed dairy context, demonstrating measurable climate adaptation benefit beyond a standard grazed pasture or post-and-wire fence baseline.

bespokePlantingRegime: Bund planted with 100% Alder/Willow (10,000 SPH) for soil stabilization. Swale sown with wet-meadow grass mix.

airFiltration.biologicalFunction: Zero woody biomass; grass sward does not effectively intercept airborne PM2.5 aerosols.

catchmentHydrology.hydrologicalFunction: Engineered physical earthworks designed to hold massive peak flood volumes.

waterPurification.biologicalJustification: Filters surface sediment well, but lacks deep root nitrogen uptake and canopy shade.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Long, unbroken sloping arable fields (Land Classes 2–4) with overland surface runoff and rill erosion risk. Contour swales are most effective on slopes of 2–8° where laser-graded earthworks can direct and detain flow. Priority: fields with documented gully formation, compacted headlands, or watercourse siltation downstream.
registry.plantingRegime: Zoned earthworks planting. Bund (upper edge, 2m width): 100% SRC Alder (Alnus glutinosa) and Willow (Salix viminalis mix) at 10,000 SPH for immediate root stabilisation of the constructed bund. Swale (6m detention zone): sown wet-meadow grass mix — Meadowsweet, Flote-grass, Yorkshire Fog, Red Fescue — to maximise roughness and FIO trapping. Bund plants: 1-year whip, bare-root. Swale seed: 15 kg/ha.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: Laser-grade contour bund and swale earthworks. Seed swale immediately after grading; plant bund whips in next planting season (Oct–March). Year 2–3: Spot weed bund plants; suppress competitive vegetation around whip bases. Long-term: Alder/Willow bund maintained as permanent uncut feature. Swale mown annually (August) and biomass removed to prevent P saturation. Inspect bund integrity and repair any rill breaches annually.
registry.rootArchitecture: Biodrilling & Pumping (bund): Alder/Willow roots on the bund penetrate 40–80cm into the constructed earthwork, providing structural stability against slump and piping failure. Alder Frankia N-fixation enriches bund soils. Willow lateral mats bind the outer bund face against spate scour. Swale: Dense rhizomatous and fibrous grass roots (0–15cm) bind the swale base, resisting scouring from ponded flow events.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Ecotone Creation: The wet-to-dry transition between swale (seasonally inundated) and bund (moist but free-draining) creates a species-rich microhabitat mosaic. Wet meadow swale supports aquatic invertebrates, frogs, and dragonflies. Alder/Willow bund provides pollen and winter cover for bumble bees and overwintering beneficial insects.
registry.waterImpacts: Runoff Interception and Detention: Engineered contour bund physically intercepts high-velocity overland flow at the slope mid-point, routing water into the detention swale. Swale holds water for 6–48 hours during storm events, allowing sediment sedimentation and infiltration. Completely disconnects the overland flow hydrological pathway before water exits the productive field. SEPA NFM alignment: modelled peak flow reduction 20–40% at field scale.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Blended carbon profile: Alder/Willow bund uses WCC SRC/wetland carbon profile (soil and root only; above-ground not harvested in this variant); wet-meadow swale uses WCS SOC accumulation profile. Combined gives 52 tCO2e/ha over 50 years. Mandatory 15% permanence buffer deducted.
registry.agronomicUtility: Erosion Control: Arrests topsoil loss down long Fife slopes, retaining N/P fertiliser in the upper field. Prevents gully formation that damages machinery and reduces field area. Swale surface provides access track for field operations during dry weather. Alder N-fixation on bund enriches adjacent field soil over time.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: SEPA NFM (Natural Flood Management) alignment. AECS Water Margin and targeted capital works eligible. EFA Buffer Strip compliant for swale area. SEPA GBR Schedule 4 compliance.
registry.additionality: Engineered Flow Disconnection: Active topographic re-engineering with laser-graded bund and detention swale physically verified via LiDAR survey, demonstrating measurable overland flow disconnection beyond standard flat margin or grass buffer baseline.

bespokePlantingRegime: 100% Short-Rotation Forestry (SRF). Sycamore, Alder, Eucalyptus (where permitted). High tonnage volumetric yield.

catchmentHydrology.hydrologicalFunction: Fast-growing root systems improve infiltration, but frequent harvesting reduces above-ground physical damming.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Heavy, wet clay soils; NVZ hotspots with extreme nitrate leaching.
registry.plantingRegime: 8 Rows. Mix: 70% SRC Willow, 30% Fast Alder. Density: 16 plants/m (staggered 1.5m x 0.5m). SPH: 14000.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: High weed suppression. Long-term: 3-year mechanical coppice. Biomass: Woodchip for local malt co-ops.
registry.rootArchitecture: Bio-Pumping & Biodrilling: Alder anchors fracture deep clay; Willow lateral mats create massive interception net.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Cover Hub: Dense cover for amphibians. Early pollen (Willow catkins) for emerging queen bumblebees.
registry.waterImpacts: Nitrate Drink: Active uptake of leached N. Transpiration dries heavy clay, extending grazing season.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: WCC short-rotation sawtooth curve. Assumes high below-ground SOC pump with 100% above-ground biomass removal every 3-5y. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~4m - 6m (Sawtooth Coppice). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Height fluctuates via harvest cycle. Averaged 3m windbreak value applied.
registry.agronomicUtility: Fuel Savings & Soil Health: Drying effect saves £70–£209/ha in fuel. Generates Nitrate Credits (£).
registry.regulatoryAlignment: EFA Buffer. ONCRA / Nitrate Credit Union verifiable.
registry.additionality: Nutrient Extraction: 3-year mechanical harvest physically removes captured N/P vs a passive buffer.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Heavy clays; NVZ hotspots; areas requiring maximum biomass volume and wind shelter.
registry.plantingRegime: 6 Rows. Mix: 70% Sycamore (Acer pseudoplatanus), 30% Poplar (Populus spp.). Density: 2m x 2m spacing. SPH: ~2500.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: Establishment. Years 2-6: Formative pruning only. Year 6-7: First clear-fell. Long-term: 6-7 year minimum rotation. Critical: minimum 6-year rotation is required to permit air-drying of harvested timber to <20% MC in Scottish lowland conditions. Harvest: stems felled autumn, field-dried through summer, chipped following autumn. Species: Birch 60% (fast growth; excellent pyrolysis char yield); Alder 30% (N-fixation; good char); Wild Cherry 10% (structure and biodiversity). Biochar application to adjacent arable at 5-10 t/ha per 10-year cycle. Farm-scale Kon-Tiki or ring kiln most practical for strip-scale volumes.
registry.rootArchitecture: Deep Anchors & Bio-Pumps: Massive lateral and taproot systems actively fracture deep plough pans and transpire vast amounts of heavy clay moisture.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Canopy Corridor: Provides significant thermal shelter for livestock and nesting canopies for larger avian predators (e.g., Buzzards, Owls) for rodent control.
registry.waterImpacts: Extreme Bio-Pumping: Rapid transpiration system designed to "drink" wet clays, while the harvested biochar acts as an ultimate flood-delay sponge when applied to soil.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Woodland Carbon Code (WCC) Native Broadleaf/Pine Yield Classes. Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~4m - 6m (Sawtooth Coppice). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Height fluctuates via harvest cycle. Averaged 3m windbreak value applied.
registry.agronomicUtility: Biochar Soil Amendment: Application at 5-10 t/ha per decade improves soil water retention (+10-15% available water capacity), cation exchange capacity, and mycorrhizal activity. Estimated additional permanence: 0.5-2.0 tCO2e/ha/yr via biochar soil pathway. Fuel savings from root-mediated compaction reduction. Generates ONCRA SSER units from combined above-ground, soil carbon, and biochar amendment pathways. Viability requires minimum 6-year rotation and verified <20% MC at time of pyrolysis.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: FGS Small Woods / EFA Buffer eligible.
registry.additionality: Industrial Carbon Removal: Physical carbon extraction verified via weighbridge metrics, proving undeniable additionality for ONCRA/Puro.earth.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Coastal and estuarine riparian zones in the East Neuk Coast biome subject to saltwater spray and storm surge risk. Exposed burn and ditch banks on free-draining sandy loams and coastal clays. Priority: unstable banks within 500m of coast where erosion threatens productive coastal agricultural land.
registry.plantingRegime: Zoned Planting. Zone 1 (sacrificial): High-density Willow. Zone 2 (asset): Sessile Oak, Alder. SPH: ~1600 overall.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1-3: Heavy duty guarding in Zone 2. Long-term: Sacrificial Zone 1 left for beaver browse; Zone 2 managed as long-term timber/carbon asset.
registry.rootArchitecture: Structural Nets: Willow creates rapid, dense bank binding; Oak provides permanent structural anchors against extreme spate.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Habitat Mosaic: Combines highly disturbed, coppiced habitat (beaver driven) with mature, undisturbed canopy.
registry.waterImpacts: Spate Management: Beaver activity in Zone 1 increases hydrological roughness, slowing flow and recharging groundwater. Note: on small watercourses prone to summer drying (e.g. small burns in the East Neuk Coast biome), mature riparian woodland can increase evapotranspiration demand and may exacerbate low-flow conditions. Where summer low-flow is the primary concern, leaky woody dams, upstream infiltration interventions, and treelines set back from the burn edge are preferred over dense riparian canopy planting on the burn bank itself (SAC agronomist review, April 2026).
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Woodland Carbon Code (WCC) Native Broadleaf/Pine Yield Classes. Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~15m - 25m (Mature Canopy). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Maximum field-scale windbreak (150m+ protection). Extreme E-W shadow drag penalty applied at Scottish latitudes.
registry.agronomicUtility: Conflict Mitigation: Concentrates beaver activity at the water's edge, physically buffering highly productive arable land from browsing damage.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: FGS Riparian Woodland (with beaver mitigation adaptations).
registry.additionality: Monitored Co-Existence: Verified zoning protocol demonstrating proactive natural capital management within complex beaver territories.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Riparian zones; floodplain burns; coastal brooks at severe risk of winter spate erosion.
registry.plantingRegime: Multi-Row Core (10+ rows). Mix: 30% Aspen, 30% Sessile Oak, 20% Downy Birch, 20% Alder. Density: 2m x 2m spacing. SPH: 1600.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1-3: Spot weeding, deer/vole guards. Long-term: Zero intervention. 30-year lock. Biomass: Natural senescence; trunks fall into river for "Instream Woody Debris".
registry.rootArchitecture: Structural Nets: Interlocking deep root systems (Oak/Aspen) physically bind the riverbank, providing massive "spate armor" against extreme winter flows.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Thermal Cooler: Dense 20m canopy provides the required shading to keep water temperatures below the critical 16°C salmonid egg survival threshold.
registry.waterImpacts: Flood Sponge: Extreme physical roughness slows over-land flow, significantly delaying downstream spate and forcing groundwater recharge. Note: on small watercourses prone to summer drying (e.g. small burns in the East Neuk Coast biome), mature riparian woodland can increase evapotranspiration demand and may exacerbate low-flow conditions. Where summer low-flow is the primary concern, leaky woody dams, upstream infiltration interventions, and treelines set back from the burn edge are preferred over dense riparian canopy planting on the burn bank itself (SAC agronomist review, April 2026).
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Woodland Carbon Code (WCC) Native Broadleaf/Pine Yield Classes. Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~15m - 25m (Mature Canopy). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Maximum field-scale windbreak (150m+ protection). Extreme E-W shadow drag penalty applied at Scottish latitudes.
registry.agronomicUtility: Flood Risk Bond: Avoids downstream property/infrastructure damage. Prevents the total loss of productive agricultural land to bank collapse.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: FGS Riparian Woodland. Long-term N-sink covenant.
registry.additionality: Verified Floodplain Sponge: Permanent land-use change with zero tillage and verified 30-year natural capital bond structure.

registry.plantingRegime: 2 Rows. Mix: 50% Hawthorn, 20% Blackthorn, 15% Hazel, 10% Field Maple, 5% Crab Apple. Density: 6 plants/m (staggered, 40-50cm apart). SPH: ~20000.

registry.waterImpacts: Intercepts Runoff: Fractures mechanical resistance in the top 30cm of clay soils, reducing surface ponding.

registry.plantingRegime: 100% Sown. Mix: 40% Cocksfoot, 30% Timothy, 20% Creeping Red Fescue, 10% Red Clover. Density: 25 kg/ha. SPH: N/A. 6m width provides enhanced sediment trapping capacity over the standard 3m Grass Silt Buffer.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: Spot spray/weed-wipe as required. Long-term: Annual mow and bale after 15 August (to protect nesting birds). Biomass must be removed from site to export captured N/P. Do not mulch in situ. Cutting height: 7–10cm to maintain dense tussock base.
registry.rootArchitecture: Fibrous: Hyper-dense shallow root mat (0–20cm) creates a physical particle net binding topsoil. Red Clover provides mild taproot aeration to 30cm. Strictly shallow profile — no risk to buried tile drainage infrastructure.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Ground Nesting Hub: Tussocky grass base supports Skylark, Grey Partridge. IPM: Cocksfoot tussocks provide year-round overwintering habitat for predatory ground beetles (Carabidae) and rove beetles controlling slugs and aphids.
registry.waterImpacts: Enhanced Sediment Trap: Double the width of standard Grass Silt Buffer increases particle residence time and trapping capacity. High surface roughness slows overland flow, dropping suspended solids and phosphorus-bearing particles before reaching the watercourse. Annual biomass removal prevents P saturation of the sward.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Hedgerow Carbon Code baseline. Fast early establishment curve managed via rotational trimming. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~4m - 6m (Dense Porous Shrub). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Optimal porous windbreak. 50-75m protection. Reduced shadow compared to mature trees.
registry.agronomicUtility: Infrastructure Protection: Physically prevents topsoil from choking field drainage networks. Keeps headlands firm and accessible for machinery. Wider footprint provides greater protection zone for tile drain outfalls.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Water Margin (6m payment tier). Satisfies SEPA GBR Schedule 4 enhanced buffer requirement. EFA Buffer Strip eligible.
registry.additionality: Enhanced Width: 6m width with mandatory annual biomass removal proves active N/P export beyond the minimum 4m regulatory requirement and passive rotting baseline.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Arable field margins and ditch edges where sub-surface tile drainage infrastructure is present. Suitable for Land Classes 1–4.2. Specifically designed for locations where deep-rooting species must be avoided to protect buried drains.
registry.plantingRegime: 2 Rows (staggered). Mix: 40% Goat Willow (Salix caprea), 30% Grey Willow (Salix cinerea), 30% Guelder Rose (Viburnum opulus). Density: 3 plants/m staggered. SPH: ~4,000. Water-tolerant species selected for wet margin compatibility.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1–2: Spot weed and mulch mat for establishment. Year 3: Remove mulch mats. Long-term: 3–4 year rotational side-trim (December–February) to maintain structural density at 1.5–2.5m height. Biomass: Chipped in situ or removed. No root disturbance — avoid deep cultivation adjacent to established plants.
registry.rootArchitecture: Shallow Fibrous: Goat and Grey Willow produce dense lateral root mats (0–30cm) specifically designed to avoid penetrating deep buried drainage assets. Root spread does not exceed 0.5–0.7× canopy width at managed heights. Guelder Rose fibrous root system remains entirely within the upper soil horizon. Safe stand-off from tile drain runs verified at standard 1m spacing.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Pollinator Hub: Goat Willow provides the earliest high-quality pollen source for emerging queen bumblebees (March). Guelder Rose provides late-season red berries for fieldfares and redwings. Willow catkins support >50 invertebrate species.
registry.waterImpacts: Silt Filter: Dense multi-stem base creates physical turbulence trapping overland flow particles. Willow root mats bind ditch and field margin soil, preventing bank slumping and reducing turbidity. Does not intercept subsurface nitrate flows (shallow roots).
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Woodland Carbon Code (WCC) Native Broadleaf/Pine Yield Classes. Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Zero aerodynamic windbreak value; zero shadow drag.
registry.agronomicUtility: Infrastructure Protection: Delivers biodiversity compliance without risk to expensive tile drainage networks. Maintains field edge trafficability. Willow provides modest biomass resource if height is managed to permit harvesting.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Biodiversity Strip compliant. Suitable as EFA Buffer Strip. Species mix approved under NatureScot native species list.
registry.additionality: Drain-Safe Biodiversity: Proves >3 native species planted at verified safe stand-off distance from sub-surface drainage infrastructure, beyond what a standard fallow or bare margin baseline provides.

registry.plantingRegime: 2 Rows (staggered). Mix: 40% Hazel (Corylus avellana), 20% Elder (Sambucus nigra), 20% Dog Rose (Rosa canina), 20% Blackthorn (Prunus spinosa). Density: 3 plants/m. SPH: ~4,500. Focus on late-season fruiting and nectar-rich species for maximum foraging value.

registry.rootArchitecture: Heart-Roots: Elder and Hazel develop mid-depth heart-root systems (30–60cm) stabilising field margins without penetrating drainage infrastructure. Dog Rose and Blackthorn provide dense fibrous surface mat (0–20cm) binding headland soils and preventing rill erosion at field edges.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Foraging & Nesting Hub: Hazel provides high-protein nuts for dormice and squirrels; Blackthorn provides the primary early nectar source for overwintered bumblebees and solitary bees; Dog Rose provides dense thorny nesting structure for Yellowhammer and Linnet; Elder flowers and berries support over 70 invertebrate species and provide critical autumn food for migratory thrushes.
registry.waterImpacts: Field Margin Stabilisation: Dense basal multi-stem structure slows overland flow, intercepting coarse sediment and reducing rill erosion at field boundaries. Moderate N/P uptake through seasonal growth cycle. Not a primary nitrate interception feature — for dissolved nitrate management install adjacent to Variant 8 or 11.

Dense structural base provides pest regulation habitat for ground beetles, parasitic wasps, and hoverflies, suppressing aphid and slug pressure in adjacent arable.

registry.soilTypeCondition: NVZ hotspots; riparian ditch proximity, heavy clay-loams prone to leaching.
registry.plantingRegime: Triple-row. Mix: 40% Hawthorn, 30% Elder, 30% Common Reed (base). Density: 6 plants/m (staggered). SPH: ~6800.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: High weed suppression. Years 2+: 3-year mechanical coppice cycle. Harvest output: woodchip for farm boilers, district heat networks, or malt co-op supply. Note: fresh SRC willow at harvest carries 50-60% moisture. Pyrolysis (biochar) requires <20% MC, which is not reliably achievable on a 3-year cycle in the Scottish maritime climate without dedicated drying infrastructure. This variant produces combustion biomass fuel only. For biochar production see Variant 18 (minimum 6-year rotation required).
registry.rootArchitecture: Bio-Filter: Triple-row density creates a dense subsurface root wall; Elder heart-roots stabilize mid-depths while reeds intercept surface flow.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Filter Hub: Focus on aquatic insect host species. Dense basal structure provides cover for amphibians and nesting birds.
registry.waterImpacts: High N-Extraction: Rapid nutrient uptake from field runoff, serving as a physical and biological barrier protecting the ditch.

Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~4m - 6m (Dense Porous Shrub). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Optimal porous windbreak. 50-75m protection. Reduced shadow compared to mature trees.
registry.agronomicUtility: Fuel Savings & Soil Health: Root drying effect saves £70-£209/ha in tractor fuel. Biomass combustion yield 8-12 t DM/ha/yr. Revenue from biomass fuel supply to farm boiler or local network. Generates Nitrate Credits via N/P extraction. Carbon: fuel substitution credit accrues to energy system (not land carbon account).
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Water Margin / SEPA Security.
registry.additionality: Active Export: Verified annual N-export audit vs standard passive grass buffer, proving direct nutrient removal.

bespokePlantingRegime: Heavy nutrient pump species. 50% Alder (nitrogen fixing/trapping), 50% Willow. 3000 SPH. Wet soil tolerance.

airFiltration.biologicalFunction: Moderate-dense leaf area from Alder/Willow canopy; effective local air filtration.

catchmentHydrology.hydrologicalFunction: Designed for subsurface water interception and aquifer recharge rather than surface flood attenuation. Wet soil conditions maximise root-zone N/P uptake.

waterPurification.biologicalJustification: Maximum purification: Alder Frankia N-fixation cycles atmospheric N, Willow deep roots pump subsurface nitrate. In NVZ context this is the highest water quality intervention in the database. Mature canopy shading reduces algal growth.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Riparian margins, seasonally wet field corners, and ditch-adjacent positions with high nitrate loading from adjacent arable land. NVZ-designated land. Suitable for gleyed soils, wet clay-loams, and permanently moist alluvial soils where standing water is present for at least part of the year. Not suitable for freely-draining soils where wet-soil Alder will fail to establish.
registry.plantingRegime: 2 Rows (staggered). Mix: 50% Alder (Alnus glutinosa), 50% Willow (Salix viminalis or Salix fragilis). Density: 3,000 SPH. Spacing: 1m x 0.6m staggered. Select Fife local provenance Alder where available. Wet-soil tolerant planting stock essential — containerised or bare-root from specialist native nursery.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: Weed suppression around stems; no cultivation. Year 2–3: Remove dead stock and replace failures. Long-term: No routine harvesting required. Feature is managed as a permanent nitrate interception buffer. Optional management: light coppice of willow every 7–10 years if height management required for adjacent field operations. Alder is NOT harvested — permanent canopy maintained for maximum N-fixation. Biomass from any willow coppice: chipped for local biomass use.
registry.rootArchitecture: Deep Lateral N-Fixers: Alder hosts Frankia alni actinobacteria in root nodules throughout the shallow and mid-depth soil profile (0–60cm), fixing 40–150 kg atmospheric nitrogen per hectare per year. Willow develops extensive lateral root mats (0–40cm) with high transpiration capacity, actively pumping excess soil moisture and dissolved nitrate from the saturated root zone. Combined root system creates a persistent biological barrier intercepting subsurface nitrate leachate pathways.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Wetland Edge Habitat: Alder and Willow provide year-round riparian structure — Willow catkins are the primary early-season pollen source for bumblebees; Alder provides cone seed for siskins and redpolls; wet soil conditions support water vole, frog, newt, and aquatic invertebrate populations. Canopy shading of the adjacent watercourse helps maintain summer water temperature below critical thresholds for aquatic life.
registry.waterImpacts: Maximum Nitrate Interception: Alder root nodule N-fixation prevents net nitrate leaching from the buffer zone itself; Willow lateral roots actively pump dissolved nitrate from subsurface leachate pathways; wet soil denitrification by anaerobic bacteria in the saturated root zone converts residual nitrate to nitrogen gas. Combined mechanisms achieve near-100% nitrate interception efficiency in the riparian position (sepaMetrics nitrateRetention=100). This is the highest-performance NVZ compliance feature in the database.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: WCC SRC/wet-woodland profile. Permanent Alder root and SOC accumulation; above-ground Alder biomass not harvested. N-fixation provides additional SOC via soil organic nitrogen pathway. Mandatory 15% permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland GDD profile applied.
registry.agronomicUtility: NVZ Compliance: Primary NVZ nitrate compliance feature. Generates Nitrate Credits via verified N-uptake and denitrification. Reduces risk of SEPA enforcement action. Supports WFD Good Ecological Status progression for the Dreel Burn. Adjacent soil N-fixation reduces fertiliser requirement on adjoining fields over time.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Water Margin / Riparian Buffer Strip. SEPA NVZ compliance tool. ONCRA Nitrate Credit Union eligible. Aligns with WFD Article 11 Programme of Measures for NVZ catchments.
registry.additionality: Biological N-Interception: Frankia-mediated atmospheric nitrogen fixation provides verified biological N removal mechanism entirely absent from standard grass margin or fallow baseline. Denitrification in saturated root zone provides additional N-removal pathway with verifiable soil moisture monitoring.

bespokePlantingRegime: 3-Tier Climax Canopy. 60% Canopy (Scots Pine, Sessile Oak, Sycamore), 20% Understory (Rowan, Hazel), 20% Shrub Edge. 2500 SPH.

registry.waterImpacts: High N-Sink: Deep roots intercept leached nitrates, acting as a permanent, long-term sequestration sponge for field runoff.

SPH: ~350–400. Wide spacing essential to maximise individual crown development and ground-level light penetration to maintain pasture productivity beneath canopy.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1–5: Vole guards, tree stakes, and spot weed suppression around base. Year 3–7: Formative pruning to develop high crown (clear stem to 2–2.5m) ensuring unimpeded airflow and livestock access to shade without bark damage. Long-term: Annual inspection for storm damage; remove epicormic growth from lower stem. Biomass: Timber asset managed under long-term schedule; no routine harvest. Root-blading optional at Year 10+ if pasture productivity under canopy requires renovation.
registry.rootArchitecture: Deep Taproots: Oak develops a primary taproot to 1.5m+ depth in well-structured soils, bypassing the shallow grass root zone and drawing deep groundwater via hydraulic redistribution during summer drought. Sycamore develops a heart-root system to 80–100cm. Hornbeam lateral roots provide mid-depth pasture stabilisation. Combined deep root architecture maximises hydraulic lift to maintain adjacent pasture soil moisture during the critical July–August drought period in the East Neuk.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Thermogenesis Corridor: Mature oak canopy supports 280+ associated invertebrate species — the highest of any native tree. Hollow trunks and dead wood in mature trees provide bat roost opportunities. Open-grown sycamore provides significant aphid resource for insectivorous birds. Connects isolated pasture habitats for barn owls, bats, and avian predators hunting rodents in the field margin.
registry.waterImpacts: Drought Buffering: Deep taproot hydraulic redistribution maintains pasture soil moisture during summer drought, reducing crop and pasture stress (see droughtResilience score=80). Canopy cooling (3.75°C below ambient on hot days; Burschel & Huss 1997) reduces evapotranspiration from the shaded zone and reduces livestock heat stress. Shade also reduces soil moisture loss through surface evaporation in the shade zone.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: WCC native broadleaf yield class profile (Sessile Oak/Sycamore mix). Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% permanence buffer deducted. Open-grown individual trees produce higher per-tree carbon stocks than woodland density, but strip-scale carbon is lower per metre than closed-canopy woodland at equivalent width. GDD: standard Scottish lowland profile.
registry.agronomicUtility: Animal Health and Productivity: Shade access reduces dairy cattle heat stress above Thermal Humidity Index 22°C, maintaining milk yield and reproductive performance during summer. Documented productivity improvements of 5–15% in milk yield on hot days when shade is available (AHDB Dairy heat stress guidance). Reduces somatic cell counts associated with heat stress mastitis risk. Long-term timber asset from Oak.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: AECS Agroforestry creation grant eligible. UKWAS pasture with trees category (where canopy cover <20%). Long-term WCC registration possible for individual trees once canopy area reaches minimum threshold.
registry.additionality: Active Drought Adaptation: Open-grown deep taproot species providing hydraulic redistribution in an exposed dairy context, demonstrating measurable climate adaptation benefit beyond a standard grazed pasture or post-and-wire fence baseline.

bespokePlantingRegime: Bund planted with 100% Alder/Willow (10,000 SPH) for soil stabilization. Swale sown with wet-meadow grass mix.

airFiltration.biologicalFunction: Zero woody biomass; grass sward does not effectively intercept airborne PM2.5 aerosols.

catchmentHydrology.hydrologicalFunction: Engineered physical earthworks designed to hold massive peak flood volumes.

waterPurification.biologicalJustification: Filters surface sediment well, but lacks deep root nitrogen uptake and canopy shade.

registry.soilTypeCondition: Long, unbroken sloping arable fields (Land Classes 2–4) with overland surface runoff and rill erosion risk. Contour swales are most effective on slopes of 2–8° where laser-graded earthworks can direct and detain flow. Priority: fields with documented gully formation, compacted headlands, or watercourse siltation downstream.
registry.plantingRegime: Zoned earthworks planting. Bund (upper edge, 2m width): 100% SRC Alder (Alnus glutinosa) and Willow (Salix viminalis mix) at 10,000 SPH for immediate root stabilisation of the constructed bund. Swale (6m detention zone): sown wet-meadow grass mix — Meadowsweet, Flote-grass, Yorkshire Fog, Red Fescue — to maximise roughness and FIO trapping. Bund plants: 1-year whip, bare-root. Swale seed: 15 kg/ha.
registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: Laser-grade contour bund and swale earthworks. Seed swale immediately after grading; plant bund whips in next planting season (Oct–March). Year 2–3: Spot weed bund plants; suppress competitive vegetation around whip bases. Long-term: Alder/Willow bund maintained as permanent uncut feature. Swale mown annually (August) and biomass removed to prevent P saturation. Inspect bund integrity and repair any rill breaches annually.
registry.rootArchitecture: Biodrilling & Pumping (bund): Alder/Willow roots on the bund penetrate 40–80cm into the constructed earthwork, providing structural stability against slump and piping failure. Alder Frankia N-fixation enriches bund soils. Willow lateral mats bind the outer bund face against spate scour. Swale: Dense rhizomatous and fibrous grass roots (0–15cm) bind the swale base, resisting scouring from ponded flow events.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Ecotone Creation: The wet-to-dry transition between swale (seasonally inundated) and bund (moist but free-draining) creates a species-rich microhabitat mosaic. Wet meadow swale supports aquatic invertebrates, frogs, and dragonflies. Alder/Willow bund provides pollen and winter cover for bumble bees and overwintering beneficial insects.
registry.waterImpacts: Runoff Interception and Detention: Engineered contour bund physically intercepts high-velocity overland flow at the slope mid-point, routing water into the detention swale. Swale holds water for 6–48 hours during storm events, allowing sediment sedimentation and infiltration. Completely disconnects the overland flow hydrological pathway before water exits the productive field. SEPA NFM alignment: modelled peak flow reduction 20–40% at field scale.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Blended carbon profile: Alder/Willow bund uses WCC SRC/wetland carbon profile (soil and root only; above-ground not harvested in this variant); wet-meadow swale uses WCS SOC accumulation profile. Combined gives 52 tCO2e/ha over 50 years. Mandatory 15% permanence buffer deducted.
registry.agronomicUtility: Erosion Control: Arrests topsoil loss down long Fife slopes, retaining N/P fertiliser in the upper field. Prevents gully formation that damages machinery and reduces field area. Swale surface provides access track for field operations during dry weather. Alder N-fixation on bund enriches adjacent field soil over time.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: SEPA NFM (Natural Flood Management) alignment. AECS Water Margin and targeted capital works eligible. EFA Buffer Strip compliant for swale area. SEPA GBR Schedule 4 compliance.
registry.additionality: Engineered Flow Disconnection: Active topographic re-engineering with laser-graded bund and detention swale physically verified via LiDAR survey, demonstrating measurable overland flow disconnection beyond standard flat margin or grass buffer baseline.

bespokePlantingRegime: 100% Short-Rotation Forestry (SRF). Sycamore, Alder, Eucalyptus (where permitted). High tonnage volumetric yield.

registry.waterImpacts: Nitrate Drink: Active uptake of leached N. Transpiration dries heavy clay, extending grazing season.

Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Height fluctuates via harvest cycle. Averaged 3m windbreak value applied.
registry.agronomicUtility: Fuel Savings & Soil Health: Drying effect saves £70–£209/ha in fuel. Generates Nitrate Credits (£).
registry.regulatoryAlignment: EFA Buffer. ONCRA / Nitrate Credit Union verifiable.

registry.maintenanceRegime: Year 1: Establishment. Years 2-6: Formative pruning only. Year 6-7: First clear-fell. Long-term: 6-7 year minimum rotation. Critical: minimum 6-year rotation is required to permit air-drying of harvested timber to <20% MC in Scottish lowland conditions. Harvest: stems felled autumn, field-dried through summer, chipped following autumn. Species: Birch 60% (fast growth; excellent pyrolysis char yield); Alder 30% (N-fixation; good char); Wild Cherry 10% (structure and biodiversity). Biochar application to adjacent arable at 5-10 t/ha per 10-year cycle. Farm-scale Kon-Tiki or ring kiln most practical for strip-scale volumes.
registry.rootArchitecture: Deep Anchors & Bio-Pumps: Massive lateral and taproot systems actively fracture deep plough pans and transpire vast amounts of heavy clay moisture.
registry.biodiversityImpacts: Canopy Corridor: Provides significant thermal shelter for livestock and nesting canopies for larger avian predators (e.g., Buzzards, Owls) for rodent control.
registry.waterImpacts: Extreme Bio-Pumping: Rapid transpiration system designed to "drink" wet clays, while the harvested biochar acts as an ultimate flood-delay sponge when applied to soil.
registry.carbonAgronomicAssumptions: Woodland Carbon Code (WCC) Native Broadleaf/Pine Yield Classes. Standard 30-year peak curve. Mandatory 15% WCC/WCS permanence buffer deducted. Standard Scottish lowland Growth Degree Days (GDD) profile applied. Agronomic Profile: Managed height ~4m - 6m (Sawtooth Coppice). Prevailing risk: Damaging Spring Easterlies (Haar). Height fluctuates via harvest cycle. Averaged 3m windbreak value applied.
registry.agronomicUtility: Biochar Soil Amendment: Application at 5-10 t/ha per decade improves soil water retention (+10-15% available water capacity), cation exchange capacity, and mycorrhizal activity. Estimated additional permanence: 0.5-2.0 tCO2e/ha/yr via biochar soil pathway. Fuel savings from root-mediated compaction reduction. Generates ONCRA SSER units from combined above-ground, soil carbon, and biochar amendment pathways. Viability requires minimum 6-year rotation and verified <20% MC at time of pyrolysis.
registry.regulatoryAlignment: FGS Small Woods / EFA Buffer eligible.
registry.additionality: Industrial Carbon Removal: Physical carbon extraction verified via weighbridge metrics, proving undeniable additionality for ONCRA/Puro.earth.
